subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 1! In this unit you will become an expert on all the numbers from 0 to 1000. Last year you mastered the numbers up to 100. Now you are going to stretch that skill ten times further, all the way to one thousand. Three-digit numbers are all around you every single day. When Amina counts that there are 365 days until the next Eid, when Yusuf reads a book with 180 pages, or when Hassan lives at house number 321 on a busy Hargeisa street, they are all using numbers to 1000.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Mathematics 0096 — Stage 3 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: This unit is designed for self-paced learning. That means you move at your own speed. Read each explanation slowly, try every worked example yourself on paper, and only move on when you feel ready. Your AI tutor is available day and night to give you extra practice, explain a step in a new way, or check your answers. Your teacher will also meet with you about twice a week to help with anything that feels tricky.

Why does this matter so much? Numbers are the language of the world around you. Traders at the Bakaara market use three-digit numbers to give the right change in shillings. Farmers use them to count their herds of goats and camels. Families use them to know how many dates to buy for the whole month of Ramadan. When you become confident with numbers to 1000, you are gaining a skill you will use every day for the rest of your life – and you are building the strong foundation you need for adding, subtracting, multiplying and dividing larger numbers in the units to come.

As you work, keep a pencil and some paper beside you. Mathematics is not a subject you can learn only by reading – you must do it. Draw the place value charts, write the numbers, and say them out loud. If you get something wrong, that is not a failure; it is simply a sign of exactly what to practise next. Every mathematician you admire once sat where you sit now, learning these very same ideas one careful step at a time.
Read, write and represent numbers from 0 to 1000.
Say and show the value of each digit in a three-digit number (hundreds, tens and ones).
Write three-digit numbers in expanded form, for example 327 = 300 + 20 + 7.
Count on and back in steps of 1, 10 and 100 from any number.
Compare two three-digit numbers and use the symbols >, < and =.
Put a set of three-digit numbers in order from smallest to greatest and greatest to smallest.
Place numbers on a number line from 0 to 1000.

concepts.title: What Is a Three-Digit Number?
concepts.explanation: A three-digit number is any number from 100 up to 999. It is made of three digits, and each digit sits in its own place: the hundreds place, the tens place and the ones place. Just like a person’s address tells you exactly which house they live in, a digit’s place tells you exactly how much it is worth. Let us look closely at the number 327. Reading from left to right:
concepts.example: The 3 is in the hundreds place, so it stands for 3 hundreds = 300.

concepts.title: Place Value – Why Position Is Everything
concepts.explanation: This is the big idea of the whole unit, so let us slow right down. The position of a digit decides its value. The very same digit can mean completely different amounts depending on where it stands. Look at the digit 5 in these three numbers:
concepts.example: In 500, the 5 is in the hundreds place, so it means five hundred.

concepts.title: Reading and Writing Numbers to 1000
concepts.explanation: When we read a three-digit number, we say the hundreds first, then the tens and ones together. We usually pop in the little word ‘and’ after the hundreds. Let us look at three examples: 327 is read as “three hundred and twenty-seven”.
concepts.example: Reading a number out loud is one of the best ways to understand it. When you say “seven hundred and eighty” you can actually hear the hundreds, and you can hear that there is nothing in the ones place. Saying numbers…

concepts.title: Counting On and Back in 1s, 10s and 100s
concepts.explanation: Counting one at a time is fine for small jumps, but slow for big ones. Smarter counting uses steps of 1, 10 and 100, and each step changes a different digit. Understanding which digit changes is the secret to counting quickly and confidently. Counting on in ones changes the ones digit: 325, 326, 327, 328, 329 … When you pass a nine, the ones roll back to 0 and the tens go up by one: 329, 330, 331.
concepts.example: Counting on in tens changes the tens digit: 240, 250, 260, 270, 280. The ones digit stays the same and the hundreds stay the same, until you pass ninety, when the tens roll to 0 and the hundreds climb: 280, 290, 300,…

concepts.title: Comparing Three-Digit Numbers
concepts.explanation: To compare two numbers means to decide which is greater, which is smaller, or whether they are equal. With three-digit numbers the method is always the same, and it always starts from the left, because the left-hand digit is worth the most. First compare the hundreds digit. The number with more hundreds is greater.
concepts.example: If the hundreds are the same, compare the tens digit.

concepts.title: Ordering Numbers and the Number Line
concepts.explanation: To order a set of numbers means to line them up by size, usually from smallest to greatest (or sometimes greatest to smallest). You use exactly the same comparing method, just applied to the whole group. Let us order these from smallest to greatest: 475, 679, 563, 621. Start with the hundreds digit of each. 475 has 4 hundreds, 563 has 5 hundreds, and both 621 and 679 have 6 hundreds. So the first two in order are 475, then 563. Now 621 and 679 both have 6 hundreds, so we look at their tens: 621 has 2 tens and 679…
concepts.example: A careful method that never fails: find the smallest number in the list and write it down, cross it off, then find the smallest of what remains, and repeat until every number is placed. Working this way stops you from…

methods.title: How to write a number in expanded form
methods.example: What is the value of the 6 in the number 462?
steps: Find the value of the hundreds digit (for example, 3 → 300).
Find the value of the tens digit (for example, 2 → 20).
Find the value of the ones digit (for example, 7 → 7).
Join them with plus signs: 300 + 20 + 7.

methods.title: How to compare two numbers
steps: Compare the hundreds digit of each number.
If one has more hundreds, that number is greater – stop.
If the hundreds are equal, compare the tens; if the tens are equal, compare the ones.
Write the correct symbol: >, < or =.

methods.title: Decide first: nearest 10 or nearest 100?
methods.example: Write ‘five hundred and four’ in digits.
steps: For the nearest 10, look at the ones digit; for the nearest 100, look at the tens digit.
If that digit is 5 or more, round up to the next ten or hundred.
If it is 4 or less, round down to the ten or hundred below.

methods.title: Comparing
steps: Solution: Both have 7 hundreds, so compare the tens.
719 has 1 ten; 791 has 9 tens.
Since 9 tens beats 1 ten, 791 > 719.
The crocodile mouth opens towards 791.

methods.title: Ordering
methods.example: Put these camel counts in order from smallest to greatest: 608, 680, 860, 806, 686.
steps: Solution: Compare hundreds first: 608, 680, 686 have 6 hundreds; 806 has 8 hundreds; 860 has 8 hundreds.
So the three 6-hundreds numbers come first.
Among 608, 680, 686 the hundreds are equal, so compare tens: 608 has 0 tens, 680 has 8 tens, 686 has 8 tens.
So 608 is first; then between 680 and 686 the tens are equal, so compare ones – 0 against 6 – so 680 then 686.

methods.title: Rounding to the nearest 10
steps: Solution: Look at the ones digit: 7.
Since 7 is 5 or more, round up.
The ten below is 900 and the next ten is 910, so 907 rounds to 910.

workedExamples.title: The value of a digit
workedExamples.prompt: What is the value of the 6 in the number 462?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Name the place first. The 6 sits in the tens place. So its value is 6 tens = 60. (The 4 is worth 400 and the 2 is worth 2, and indeed 400 + 60 + 2 = 462.)

workedExamples.title: Expanded form
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Break it into places. 8 hundreds = 800, 1 ten = 10, 9 ones = 9. So 819 = 800 + 10 + 9. Notice each part shows the true value of its digit.

workedExamples.title: Words to digits with a place holder
workedExamples.prompt: Write ‘five hundred and four’ in digits.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Listen for each place. Five hundred gives a 5 in the hundreds. There is no tens word, so the tens place needs a place-holder 0. ‘Four’ gives a 4 in the ones. The number is 504. Forgetting the zero would wrongly give 54.

workedExamples.title: Comparing
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Both have 7 hundreds, so compare the tens. 719 has 1 ten; 791 has 9 tens. Since 9 tens beats 1 ten, 791 > 719. The crocodile mouth opens towards 791.

workedExamples.title: Ordering
workedExamples.prompt: Put these camel counts in order from smallest to greatest: 608, 680, 860, 806, 686.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Compare hundreds first: 608, 680, 686 have 6 hundreds; 806 has 8 hundreds; 860 has 8 hundreds. So the three 6-hundreds numbers come first. Among 608, 680, 686 the hundreds are equal, so compare tens: 608 has 0 tens, 680 has 8 tens, 686 has 8 tens. So 608 is first; then between 680 and 686 the tens are equal, so compare ones – 0 against 6 – so 680 then 686. Now the 8-hundreds numbers: 806 has 0 tens, 860 has 6 tens, so 806 then 860. Final order: 608, 680, 686, 806, 860.

workedExamples.title: Rounding to the nearest 10
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Look at the ones digit: 7. Since 7 is 5 or more, round up. The ten below is 900 and the next ten is 910, so 907 rounds to 910.

workedExamples.title: Rounding to the nearest 100
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Look at the tens digit: 4. Since 4 is 4 or less, round down. The hundred below is 600 and the next hundred is 700, so 648 rounds to 600. (Careful: do not be fooled by the 8 in the ones – for the nearest 100 we look only at the tens.)

workedExamples.title: Estimating a sum by rounding
workedExamples.prompt: Estimate 328 + 271 by rounding each number to the nearest 10.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: 328 rounds to 330 (ones digit 8, round up). 271 rounds to 270 (ones digit 1, round down). So the estimate is 330 + 270 = 600. The real answer is 599, so our estimate is excellent – that is how we know it makes sense.

workedExamples.title: Round each to the nearest 10
workedExamples.prompt: Round each to the nearest 10: a) 23 b) 385 c) 599 d) 740.

workedExamples.title: Round each to the nearest 100
workedExamples.prompt: Round each to the nearest 100: a) 123 b) 678 c) 950.

workedExamples.title: Which number is
workedExamples.prompt: Which number is: a) 10 more than 495 b) 100 more than 275 c) 100 less than 756?

activities.title: Place Value Detective
activities.materials: paper, a pencil, and three dice (or number cards 0–9).
steps: Roll the three dice to make a three-digit number (choose an order for the digits).
Write the number down, then draw a place value chart showing hundreds, tens and ones.
Write the value of each digit, and then write the whole number in expanded form.
Write the number in words.
Repeat five times, then challenge yourself: with your three digits, make the largest and the smallest three-digit number possible.

activities.title: Build It With Bundles (Base-Ten)
activities.materials: a big pile of small stones, sticks or dry beans, and some string or rubber bands.
steps: Make some ‘hundreds’ by tying ten bundles of ten together, some ‘tens’ by tying ten singles together, and keep some loose ‘ones’.
Ask a family member to call out a three-digit number under 1000.
Build it using hundreds, tens and ones – for example, 327 is 3 hundreds, 2 tens and 7 ones.
Say the expanded form out loud: three hundred plus twenty plus seven.
Now swap two of the digits and build the new number. Talk about how much the value changed.

activities.title: The Crocodile Compare Game
activities.materials: paper cards, each with a three-digit number you write yourself (make about twenty).
steps: Shuffle the cards and place them face down in a pile.
Each player turns over one card.
Draw a crocodile mouth (> or <) pointing at the greater number, saying why using place value.
The player with the greater number keeps both cards.
Play until the cards run out. The player with the most cards wins.

activities.title: Rounding Race to the Nearest 100
activities.materials: paper, pencil, and your number cards from Activity 3.
steps: Draw a number line and mark the hundreds: 0, 100, 200 … up to 1000.
Turn over a card and place the number roughly where it belongs on the line.
Decide which hundred it is closest to, and round it there. Check with the tens-digit rule.
Do the same for rounding to the nearest 10 on a smaller line.
Keep score: one point each time you and a partner agree on the correct rounding.

activities.title: Estimation Station (Investigation)
activities.materials: a container and lots of small objects (beans, buttons, bottle caps).
steps: Put a large handful of objects in the container.
Estimate how many there are, and write your estimate down as a sensible guess.
Count them by grouping into tens, then into hundreds, then counting the groups.
Compare your estimate to the true count. How close were you?
Repeat three times with different amounts and see whether your estimates get better.

activities.title: Number Line Hop (Family Game)
activities.materials: chalk or a long strip of paper, and your family or friends.
steps: Draw a big number line on the ground from 0 to 1000, marking every 100.
Call out a three-digit number, and have a player hop to roughly where it belongs.
The others say whether it is closer to the hundred below or the hundred above.
Now call out numbers to order: three players each take a number and line themselves up smallest to greatest.
Talk about how the number line and place value always agree.

rules.title: Place Value Rule
rules.text: Left digit = hundreds. Middle digit = tens. Right digit = ones. 327 = 3 hundreds + 2 tens + 7 ones = 300 + 20 + 7. A zero is a place holder: it keeps a place empty, like the 0 in 504.

rules.title: Comparing Rule
rules.text: Compare the hundreds first. More hundreds = greater number. If the hundreds are equal, compare the tens. If the tens are equal, compare the ones. The open mouth of > or < always points at the bigger number.

rules.title: Rounding Rule
rules.text: To the nearest 10, look at the ones digit. To the nearest 100, look at the tens digit. If that digit is 5 or more, round up. If it is 4 or less, round down. Example: 648 rounds to 650 to the nearest 10, and to 600 to the nearest 100.

terms: What a digit is worth from its position
The three places in a three-digit number
A number written as the sum of its parts, like 300 + 20 + 7
A zero that keeps a place empty, like the 0 in 504
To decide which number is greater, smaller or equal
A sensible guess before counting exactly
To change a number to a nearby tidy ten or hundred
commonMistakes: The tens place needs a 0, so write 507
The 5 is in the tens place, so it means 50
Tens first: 0 tens is less than 8 tens, so 508 < 580
Tens digit is 4, so round down to 600
Round up only when the deciding digit is 5 or more

realProblems.prompt: At the Bakaara market, Sumaya buys 2 sacks of 100 mangoes, 3 bags of 10 mangoes, and 5 loose mangoes. How many mangoes in total? Write the number and its expanded form.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: Liban counts 648 cattle at the well and his cousin counts 684 at another. Which well has more, and how do you know? Round each count to the nearest 100.
realProblems.answer: The second well (684 > 648, because with equal hundreds 8 tens beats 4 tens); both round to about 600 and 700 – 648 rounds to 600, 684 rounds to 700.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: The second well (684 > 648, because with equal hundreds 8 tens beats 4 tens); both round to about 600 and 700 – 648 rounds to 600, 684 rounds to 700.

realProblems.prompt: A shopkeeper in Kismaayo lines up prices in shillings: 375, 357, 735, 573, 537. Put them in order from cheapest to most expensive.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: Noor packs water bottles into crates of 100. She has 907 bottles. How many full crates of 100 can she pack, and how many bottles are left over?
realProblems.answer: 9 full crates with 7 bottles left over.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 9 full crates with 7 bottles left over.

realProblems.prompt: During Eid, a masjid in Berbera expects about 500 people. If 462 come on the first day and 538 on the second, use rounding to estimate the total, then say whether more than 1000 people came in total.
realProblems.answer: 462 rounds to 500 and 538 rounds to 500, so the estimate is about 1000; the exact total is 462 + 538 = 1000, so exactly 1000 people came – not more than 1000.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 462 rounds to 500 and 538 rounds to 500, so the estimate is about 1000; the exact total is 462 + 538 = 1000, so exactly 1000 people came – not more than 1000.

realProblems.prompt: Which number is: a) 10 more than 495 b) 100 more than 275 c) 100 less than 756?
realProblems.hint: Represent the information, name the rule, then solve one step at a time.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Using digit cards 3, 5 and 7, make the largest and the smallest three-digit number possible. Then write each in words.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 1) Largest = 753 (seven hundred and fifty-three), smallest = 357 (three hundred and fifty-seven).

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Find the number that is between 400 and 500 with a 5 in the tens place.
A number rounds to 700 when rounded to the nearest 100. What is the smallest and the largest whole number it could be?
Sofia says every number that ends in 0 (like 100, 200, 300 … up to 1000) rounds to itself for both the nearest 10 and the nearest 100. Do you agree? Explain with an example.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Yes – a multiple of 100 is already a whole ten and a whole hundred, so it rounds to itself; for example 300 rounds to 300 both ways.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Tomas makes three-digit numbers using the digit cards 4, 7 and 3. He has made 473 and 347. Write all the other three-digit numbers he can make with these three cards, then put your full list in order from smallest to greatest.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: The other numbers are 437, 374, 743, 734; full list in order: 347, 374, 437, 473, 734, 743.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain the key idea in Ordering Numbers and the Number Line.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: To order a set of numbers means to line them up by size, usually from smallest to greatest (or sometimes greatest to smallest). You use exactly the same comparing method, just applied to the whole group. Let us order these from smallest to greatest: 475, 679, 563, 621. Start with the hundreds digit of each. 475 has 4 hundreds, 563 has 5 hundreds, and both 621 and 679 have 6 hundreds. So the first two in order are 475, then 563. Now 621 and 679 both have 6 hundreds, so we look at their tens: 621 has 2 tens and 679…

questions.question: What is the value of the 6 in the number 462?
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 1 methods.
questions.explanation: Name the place first. The 6 sits in the tens place. So its value is 6 tens = 60. (The 4 is worth 400 and the 2 is worth 2, and indeed 400 + 60 + 2 = 462.)

questions.question: Estimate 328 + 271 by rounding each number to the nearest 10.
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 1 methods.
questions.explanation: 328 rounds to 330 (ones digit 8, round up). 271 rounds to 270 (ones digit 1, round down). So the estimate is 330 + 270 = 600. The real answer is 599, so our estimate is excellent – that is how we know it makes sense.

questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.

questions.question: Round 834 to the nearest 10.

questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.

questions.question: What does “Digit” mean?
options: What a digit is worth from its position
The three places in a three-digit number
A number written as the sum of its parts, like 300 + 20 + 7
questions.hint: Use the Unit 1 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Digit means A single symbol from 0 to 9.

questions.question: What does “Place value” mean?
options: What a digit is worth from its position
The three places in a three-digit number
A number written as the sum of its parts, like 300 + 20 + 7
A zero that keeps a place empty, like the 0 in 504
questions.answer: What a digit is worth from its position
questions.hint: Use the Unit 1 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Place value means What a digit is worth from its position.

questions.question: What does “Hundreds / Tens / Ones” mean?
options: The three places in a three-digit number
A number written as the sum of its parts, like 300 + 20 + 7
A zero that keeps a place empty, like the 0 in 504
To decide which number is greater, smaller or equal
questions.answer: The three places in a three-digit number
questions.hint: Use the Unit 1 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Hundreds / Tens / Ones means The three places in a three-digit number.

games.title: Quick Match: Digit
games.description: Practise digit through four short challenges.

rounds.prompt: What is the value of the 6 in the number 462?
rounds.clue: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 1 methods.
rounds.explanation: Name the place first. The 6 sits in the tens place. So its value is 6 tens = 60. (The 4 is worth 400 and the 2 is worth 2, and indeed 400 + 60 + 2 = 462.)

rounds.clue: Work it out step by step, then check each option.
rounds.explanation: Counting on one from 577 gives 578.

rounds.clue: Work it out step by step, then check each option.
rounds.explanation: 834 rounds to 830 — look at the ones digit.

choices: A single symbol from 0 to 9

The three places in a three-digit number
A number written as the sum of its parts, like 300 + 20 + 7
rounds.clue: Use the Unit 1 Math Words & Symbols reference.
rounds.explanation: Digit means A single symbol from 0 to 9.

games.title: Concept Quest: Place value
games.description: Practise place value through four short challenges.

rounds.prompt: Estimate 328 + 271 by rounding each number to the nearest 10.
rounds.clue: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 1 methods.
rounds.explanation: 328 rounds to 330 (ones digit 8, round up). 271 rounds to 270 (ones digit 1, round down). So the estimate is 330 + 270 = 600. The real answer is 599, so our estimate is excellent – that is how we know it makes sense.

choices: They are equal
rounds.clue: Work it out step by step, then check each option.
rounds.explanation: 800 is greater than 629.

choices: They are equal
rounds.clue: Work it out step by step, then check each option.
rounds.explanation: 719 is greater than 541.

selfAssessment: I can this unit is designed for self-paced learning. That means you move at your own speed. Read each explanation slowly, try every worked example yourself on paper, and only move on when you feel ready. Your AI tutor is available day and night to give you extra practice, explain a step in a new way, or check your answers. Your teacher will also meet with you about twice a week to help with anything that feels tricky.

I can why does this matter so much? Numbers are the language of the world around you. Traders at the Bakaara market use three-digit numbers to give the right change in shillings. Farmers use them to count their herds of goats and camels. Families use them to know how many dates to buy for the whole month of Ramadan. When you become confident with numbers to 1000, you are gaining a skill you will use every day for the rest of your life – and you are building the strong foundation you need for adding, subtracting, multiplying and dividing larger numbers in the units to come.

I can as you work, keep a pencil and some paper beside you. Mathematics is not a subject you can learn only by reading – you must do it. Draw the place value charts, write the numbers, and say them out loud. If you get something wrong, that is not a failure; it is simply a sign of exactly what to practise next. Every mathematician you admire once sat where you sit now, learning these very same ideas one careful step at a time.
I can read, write and represent numbers from 0 to 1000.
I can say and show the value of each digit in a three-digit number (hundreds, tens and ones).
I can write three-digit numbers in expanded form, for example 327 = 300 + 20 + 7.
I can count on and back in steps of 1, 10 and 100 from any number.
I can compare two three-digit numbers and use the symbols >, < and =.
